purpose: Define reasoning chains that explain why TDD judgment holds.
load_condition: Load when the user asks why, or when tradeoffs in TDD judgment matter.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Tests written before implementation drive design; tests written after confirm implementation.
logic: When the test is written first, it specifies what the code should do without being influenced by how the code currently works.
This forces the developer (or agent) to think about the interface and behavior before the implementation exists.
The resulting design tends to be simpler and more testable because it was designed to be tested.
When the test is written after, it naturally mirrors the implementation because the author knows how the code works.
Therefore, test-first design produces better abstractions than test-after verification.
reasoning_chains.so_what: TDD is not just about catching bugs. Its primary value is design feedback — the test shapes the code before the code exists.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Vertical slices are independently valuable; horizontal slices are only collectively valuable.
logic: A vertical slice delivers one complete behavior: from user input to observable output.
This behavior is independently testable, independently deployable, and independently verifiable by the user.
A horizontal slice delivers one layer of the system: all unit tests, or all integration tests, or all E2E tests.
No single horizontal slice can verify that the system actually works — only the complete stack can.
Therefore, vertical slices provide value incrementally while horizontal slices only provide value when complete.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Organize work around behaviors (vertical slices), not layers. Each slice should be a complete, testable, demonstrable unit of value.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: The simplest passing implementation is correct because complexity should be driven by need, not anticipation.
logic: When only one test exists, the simplest implementation that passes it is the most honest response: it does exactly what the test demands, no more.
Adding complexity beyond what the test requires is anticipation — guessing what future tests will need.
Anticipated complexity is often wrong because the next test may demand a different generalization.
Each new test adds exactly the complexity it requires, building the design incrementally from simple to complex.
Therefore, hardcoding during GREEN is not laziness — it is discipline against premature generalization.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Don't anticipate. Let each test drive exactly the complexity it demands. The next test will tell you what to generalize.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Refactoring while RED is waste because there is no safety net to catch regressions.
logic: Refactoring means changing implementation without changing behavior.
To verify behavior hasn't changed, all tests must pass before and after the refactoring.
If tests are failing (RED), the agent cannot verify that refactoring preserved behavior.
Any regression introduced during refactoring while RED will be masked by the already-failing test.
Therefore, the only valid action during RED is to make the test pass. Refactoring must wait for GREEN.
reasoning_chains.so_what: GREEN before REFACTOR. Always. No exceptions. The cycle is RED → GREEN → REFACTOR, not RED → REFACTOR → GREEN.
